item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Truman would be proud"

Instant +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: lol

item.title: 200 updoots
item.content:encoded: glad i could be the 200th. discussion is mildy tepid here though 0-0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm disappointed the stickers mentioned on the box are not in the skip description.
Even if they are just stickers, it would be sensible step for The Foundation to test it and mention that detail.

Decals of US and USSSR flags could alter flight and detonation details to match cold war munitions from that country (while still being non-damaging and non-radioactive). The cartoon character sticker adds the effect of everyone in a blast radius getting uniform coating of carbon dust, except it doesn't get in their eyes. (idea is that they look like Wile E. Coyote after the dynamite goes off). Administration has of course strictly forbidden testing with the stickers of radiation and biohazard symbols.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most Things the Factory creates are used to harm others. If this was from the factory, it would probably be a nuke that can be reused again and again, a nuke that is powerful enough to destroy the Earth, Maybe 682, or a nuke that hurts people, not structures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Was this made because of the famous "a nuke would be considered safe class" example?

A typical Wondertainment product, maybe on a tad too big scale compared with the other toys he makes. I mean, come on, something that can cause a real nuclear war?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh? What's this? A Wondertainment toy that doesn't do any actual damage to anyone or anything provided safety precautions are taken? Perhaps I should look outside and make sure the sky is not falling, haw-hee-haw-hee-haw. XD

Joking aside, I do see the potential for disaster here, considering the sorts of mistaken assumptions that could easily be made around a thing that looks like a strike from a nuclear bomb. There's just one thing, though: "it's launched into the air at 45 meters per second, then comes back down and explodes, etc." - "45 meters per second" says nothing of how long it's in the air or how high up it goes; unless you expect people to calculate air resistance and how much it will slow down over how long a period of time before running out of momentum and fuel and falling back to Earth, that information should probably be in there somewhere.

I thought I also had a second issue when I first read the article, but I can't remember it now, so…never mind with that, I guess. In any case, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just browsing all of the SCPs, and came to this.
I bet the CI was unpleasently surpised to find out they weren't real nukes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not even an actual nuke. :-/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i wrote an SCP like this once, (before i deleted it because it was terrible)
it was a frozen nuke, on the brink of exploding
and ohyeahitcausedpeopletomutateintohorriblemonsters.
it was a good idea, but the Foundation doesn't need another nuke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd say give it "a bright light and sound similar to that emitted by a real nuke". A flash identical to a nuke flash burns people's shadows into walls.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one.

EMPs cause permanent damage to circuitry

The handwaving of 'a weak radio wave pulse similar to an EMP' works for me.

Also, strongly disagreeing with earlier comments that this is more Factory than Wondertainment. Even if it has unintentional applications that can cause serious trouble (disabling communication devices, causing chaos by imitating a real-life nuclear device), this has neither the asethetic nor the typical sidelong malicious intent of a Factory item. It feels just fine for Wondertainment to me. Just take a look at a particularly popular Wondertainment item, SCP-846. Sure, you could kill someone pretty easily with Robo-Dude, but it's seriously unlikely that that was the point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the only issue I have is the EMP effect - I know this is a Wondertainment toy, but 1) EMPs cause permanent damage to circuitry and 2) Something like this would draw too much attention. It is otherwise a pretty rad idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Until I read the end note, I thought this was stupid. Tentatively upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nitpick: I don't think "shock-absorbing ear plugs" is something that could exist. That is, ear plugs just aren't capable of keeping a shock wave from reaching the eardrums. Nor would those ear-muff type sound reducers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I disagree. Wondertainment didn't make this thing to be seen by aircraft or from a long distance away; he made it so that kids could set off a harmless nuclear explosion in their backyards. It would be made to look authentic to those kids that are standing 20 feet away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Completely disagree.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the guidance system idea (It fixes the problem of harm to the user while still having the threat of nuclear apocalypse). It'd also work great with some rather more personal stickers that would appeal to a 9-year-old ("School", "The Dentist's office," etc.). I'll try to see if I can incorporate that after classes. Thanks for your ideas and criticism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You don't really have to convince the people in the city it hits. You need to convince those who see it in flight (visual, radar, satellite), and people 20 miles away from the city who see it in the distance. Causing radio silence will convince everyone else it happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the criticism. I'll try to address it.

@Request/Denial: Changed to reflect criticism. Request is now to inform governments preemptively of SCP-1068. The O5 denial has been removed.

@Destructive Potential: I understand that a giant flashbang/temporary electronic wipeout/giant BOOM! sound is a bit over-the-top for a Wondertainment product. The key here, however, is that the object is a fake nuke that when detonated, resembles a nuclear device to both kids and world governments. Reducing the size of the light means that it'll obviously be detected as a fake. Reducing the sound is easier, but still reduces the overall illusion (Maybe the sound starts a few kilometers away and is quieter from there?). And the pulse I intentionally kept vague on the size of, since a radius of 1 kilometer isn't really that big, and a radius of 9 kilometers is pretty damn big.

Maybe it should come packaged with a pair of goggles/earplugs that block all sound/light the moment the model detonates? It would show that Wondertainment cares about the safety of the kids buying their products, while still showing a complete accidental disregard for everything else.

@Seems more Factory than Wondertainment: In my experience, factory items are generally smaller, less toy-like, and are more gruesome for the user/victim. If it still seems like a Factory item to you, please tell me what parts seem more Factory-like to you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As it is, it is mostly a cutting-edge high tech toy. If it caused less damage but emulated the LGM-30 ICBM's flight characteristics it would be more anomalous. This would make it a more valid threat to world peace. If the guidance system was controlled by which stickers you put on it ("Moscow or Bust", "Hello New York", "Sayonara Tokyo") it would be more 'Wondertainmenty'. Instead of actually exploding, if it landed and emitted a 'jamming frequency' (instead of a temporary EMP burst) it might be better. If it is not going to be reuseable make it run on solid fuel. Remember, kids: the Mini-Minuteman has three independant warheads so you can triple your fun!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seems more Factory than Wondertainment

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with the above. The power just needs to be tweaked down a little. Also, where can I get the big red button?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of an SCP that is harmless by itself, but whose use could cause a nuclear war. I second the suggestions of making it really harmless (it could blind people, but temporarily). That way, the only harm it could cause would be indirectly causing a government to retaliate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea, not the excecution. Not only for things that have already been stated, but at the bottom with the suggestions and request denied,it seems shoved in there and ends the article on a sour note. Downvoted until edited right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding what Anaxagoras said, this thing is too destructive in its current form. Each one of these things wiping out electronics for miles? Simply doesn't compute.

Here's how I'd tweak things:

The 'extremely bright light' is the size of a pinpoint. If you're looking exactly at it, it's as dangerous as a real one to someone's vision. But otherwise, it's non-harmful. But wear eye protection anyway, kids!

The sound? Loud, but not drastically so. Might give headaches.

The 'emp wipeout range'? Drastically reduced. Safe to 'bomb' on your lawn, but not inside, kids!

Right now, I have to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one, and am usually not a fan of Wondertainment stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Something else you might want to clear up: when an actual EMP fries electronics, they are quite literally fried. They don't just turn off temporarily and turn back on later like in every movie ever. You might want to specify that the electronics are only temporarily disrupted. Granted, you said radio wave, not EMP wave, so maybe you already know everything I just said and I just don't know that radio waves can temporarily disable electronics. In that case, feel free to ignore everything up there.

But in other news, you should totally make the packaging look like a missle silo. And include a plastic stand that looks like a genuine missle-launching stand thing so that kids can fire it at their neighbors rather than just sending it straight up.

Edit: Now that my childish glee has worn off and I've had time to think about it, I've realized that Wondertainment probably wouldn't include the stand. That would be very unsafe, and the last thing Wondertainment wants is for kids to be unsafe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can change the part about the government easily; I assumed it would be fun not knowing why the O5 council would deny such vital information. I was unsure of that part myself, however, so it's no huge loss. I'll wait and see what others have to say on it before making that move.

I tried to make the object "harmless" (It can deafen and blind people, and it blacks out electronics, but it doesn't have destructive force or continuous lasting radioactive decay). Dr. Wondertainment even recommends safety gear in its operation that would protect the user against harm; what they/he forgot about, of course, were the political and social ramifications of detonating a fake nuclear device.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really know where you're getting destructive from—it's pretty explicitly harmless. But I agree about the government thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Far too destructive. I dislike any Wondertainment product with that high a chance of killing the user. Also, the Foundation would have to inform governments of this. It's not like the existence of the Foundation is a secret from the world governments, this item isn't particularly important to keep secret, and a nuclear war would be far more destructive to the human race than all but a handful of SCPs. Besides, if they were really worried about it, they could just spread the word of mundane devices capable of causing EMPs and the appearance of a nuclear explosion.
